serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of chana masala rice served in a Singapore hawker or canteen context is approximately 300 g, comprising roughly 150 g of cooked rice and 150 g of chana masala curry.

nutrient_source.title: Chana Masala Rice — Singapore Health Promotion Board (HPB) Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id G00010523 is sourced directly from HPB Foodo with provenance marked canonical and confidence 0.95. All per_100g values are preserved from the HPB source record.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; University of Sydney GI Database — chickpea and basmati rice composite estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for chana masala rice as a composite dish. GI estimated at 45 (low) based on weighted average of two approved-source anchors: (1) boiled chickpeas GI ≈ 28–36 (University of Sydney GI Database); (2) basmati rice GI ≈ 50–58 (University of Sydney GI Database). A typical Singapore serving of this dish is approximately 50% rice and 50% chana masala by weight; the chickpea fraction substantially lowers the composite GI. Glycemic load per 300 g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (45 × 75.57) / 100 ≈ 34.0, rounded to 33.8 using 25.19 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 300 g serving / 100 × GI 45.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 231 mg/100 g, partially offset by potassium 327 mg/100 g and dietary fibre 3.7 g/100 g
hypertension.guidance: Sodium at 231 mg/100 g (693 mg per 300 g serving) is moderate and approaches one-third of the 2,000 mg daily limit; patients with hypertension should request reduced salt in the masala preparation and pair this dish with low-sodium sides, while the high potassium-to-sodium ratio (1.41:1) and DASH-compatible fibre content provide partial cardiovascular offset.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low composite GI (~45), high dietary fibre 3.7 g/100 g, protein 6.5 g/100 g slowing gastric emptying
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low estimated composite GI of approximately 45, driven by chickpeas (GI 28–36), combined with 3.7 g/100 g dietary fibre and 6.5 g/100 g protein, significantly moderates postprandial glycaemic response; patients with Type 2 Diabetes should control portion size to approximately 200–250 g and favour a higher chickpea-to-rice ratio to maximise fibre and protein benefit while keeping glycaemic load within target.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (~0.6 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, high dietary fibre 3.7 g/100 g, plant-based legume protein
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Chana masala rice is a plant-based dish with low saturated fat (approximately 0.6 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, and 3.7 g/100 g dietary fibre from chickpeas, which supports LDL reduction; the anti-inflammatory spice profile (turmeric, ginger) provides additional cardiovascular benefit, making this dish suitable for patients with cardiovascular disease when sodium intake is managed.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric, ginger), high fibre supporting gut microbiome, low saturated fat; nightshade ingredients present
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The dish contains evidence-based anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric/curcumin, ginger) and high-fibre chickpeas that support gut microbiome immune regulation relevant to RA; however, patients with known nightshade sensitivity should request reduced or eliminated tomato and chili, and those on NSAIDs should note that amchur (dry mango powder) may cause GI discomfort.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chana Masala Rice is a popular Indian-origin dish widely served in Singapore hawker centres, canteens, and Indian food stalls. It consists of cooked basmati or long-grain rice served alongside or mixed with chana masala — a spiced chickpea curry made from boiled chickpeas simmered in a tomato-onion-garlic-ginger base seasoned with turmeric, cumin, coriander, garam masala, red chili powder, and amchur (dry mango powder). The dish is entirely plant-based, vegan, and gluten-free. Texturally, the chickpeas are tender and absorb the aromatic masala sauce, while the rice provides a soft, neutral base. The flavour profile is earthy, mildly spicy, tangy from amchur, and warmly aromatic from the spice blend. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides 164 kcal, 6.5 g protein, 4.21 g fat, 25.19 g carbohydrate, 3.7 g dietary fibre, and 231 mg sodium. The high fibre and protein content from chickpeas gives this dish a low estimated composite glycaemic index of approximately 45, making it suitable for blood glucose management. The potassium-to-sodium ratio of 1.41:1 (327 mg potassium vs 231 mg sodium per 100 g) supports DASH-diet principles for hypertension management. The low saturated fat content (approximately 0.6 g/100 g) and zero cholesterol make it cardiovascular-friendly. Anti-inflammatory spices including turmeric (curcumin) and ginger add functional health value relevant to inflammatory conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis. In Singapore, this dish is commonly consumed at lunch and dinner and is a staple of Indian vegetarian cuisine in the local hawker context.
common_user_queries: Is chana masala rice good for diabetics?
Chana masala rice GI glycemic index

confidence_reason: All per_100g nutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database (cr_id G00010523, provenance canonical, confidence 0.95). The source match is exact. GI is estimated from approved University of Sydney GI Database anchors for chickpeas and basmati rice with documented methodology. Multilingual names are verified for the Singaporean Indian cuisine context.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient vitamins, phosphorus, zinc, selenium, and moisture data for this composite chana masala rice dish are not individually reported in the HPB Foodo source record (G00010523) and are not available in USDA FoodData Central or ICMR-NIN for this exact composite preparation as of 2026. Saturated fat estimated at 0.6 g/100 g based on ICMR-NIN chickpea curry and rice composite analogy. Cholesterol is 0.0 mg/100 g — confirmed zero per HPB source; this is consistent with the entirely plant-based formulation of the dish (naturally zero cholesterol). GI of 45 is an analogous estimate derived from University of Sydney GI Database anchors for boiled chickpeas (GI 28–36) and basmati rice (GI 50–58), weighted approximately 50:50 by dish composition; no direct GI measurement for chana masala rice as a composite dish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026. FoodOn and FoodEx2 codes are approximate matches; no exact ontology entry exists for chana masala rice as a named composite dish. Input alias contained emoji character (❤️) which has been preserved in aliases for retrieval completeness but removed from canonical food_name.
